comparisons.name: SeniorCare

diffs.found: Prescription drug and vaccine coverage. Level 1: No deductible, $5 copay for generic drugs, $15 copay for brand-name drugs. Level 2A: $500 deductible per person, then $5/$15 copays. Level 2B: $850 deductible per person, then $5/$15 copays. Level 3: Spenddown equal to income exceeding 240% FPL, then coverage at SeniorCare rates.[7]

value: Proof of Wisconsin residency (not explicitly detailed in search results)
Proof of U.S. citizenship or qualifying immigrant status (not explicitly detailed in search results)
Annual income documentation (not explicitly detailed in search results)
novelFields.note: Has document checklist — our model doesn't store per-program documents

comparisons.dataShapeNotes: SeniorCare is a prescription drug assistance program (not long-term care or medical services). Benefits scale by income tier using federal poverty level thresholds. No asset limits are mentioned. The program is statewide with centralized enrollment. Income thresholds are indexed to FPL and updated annually, making 2026 limits specific to that year. Household composition (single vs. couple) affects income thresholds but not eligibility otherwise.
gotchas: You cannot enroll if you are a full-benefit Medicaid member (BadgerCare Plus), though narrow exceptions exist for QMBs, QIs, SLMB, TB-related Medicaid, and unmet deductibles[3][4]
Annual renewal required to maintain coverage[4]
$30 annual enrollment fee per person[5][6]
Coverage begins the month *after* application, not the month of application[4][5]
Earliest application is during the calendar month you turn 65; you can apply any time after that[5][6]
Income limits are based on federal poverty level and change annually[7]
For married couples, both spouses' income counts toward eligibility and coverage level determination[3]
SeniorCare coordinates with other insurance (Medicare Part B/D) but does not replace it[4]

comparisons.name: Family Care

diffs.found: Home and Community Based Services (HCBS) via managed care organizations (CMOs), including assistance with ADLs/IADLs, long-term care to avoid nursing homes; specific services per Wisconsin Administrative Code DHS 10.41 (e.g., personal care, adult day care, respite, home modifications); no fixed dollar/hour limits—tailored to assessed needs

novelFields.value: Medicaid asset limits apply (e.g., single: <$2,000 as of 2025); countable assets include most savings/checking, investments; exempt: primary home, one vehicle, personal belongings, burial funds[2]
novelFields.note: Our model has no asset limit fields

novelFields.value: Administered by different managed care organizations (CMOs) per region; service providers, options differ by county/service area; some areas offer Family Care Partnership or PACE instead/alongside
novelFields.note: Program varies by region — our model doesn't capture this
Has waitlist info — our model has no wait time field

value: Proof of income/assets for Medicaid financial assessment
novelFields.note: Has document checklist — our model doesn't store per-program documents

comparisons.dataShapeNotes: Regionally administered via multiple managed care organizations (CMOs) with county-specific availability; eligibility ties directly to Medicaid standards plus functional screen; no waitlists but not uniform statewide
gotchas: Not statewide—must confirm county availability; ineligible if outside service area
Requires both functional (NFLOC via screen) AND Medicaid financial eligibility; apply only if likely to qualify to avoid denial
Family Care Partnership is a variant for those dually eligible for Medicare/Medicaid, with different regions
Ongoing eligibility requires remaining in service area and re-meeting criteria
Services depend on chosen CMO and care plan—not automatic

comparisons.name: IRIS (Include, Respect, I Self-Direct)

diffs.found: Self-directed long-term care services via personalized IRIS budget (covers supports like personal care, goods/services to meet long-term care needs; participant is employer, uses Fiscal Employment Agent (FEA) for payroll/taxes; specific services listed by DHS, no fixed dollar/hour amounts—budget based on assessed needs)[3][6][7].

novelFields.value: Medicaid asset limits apply (standard Medicaid rules, e.g., home equity limit of $750,000 in 2025 if living in home or intent to return; exemptions for primary home if spouse or minor/disabled child lives there)[2].
novelFields.note: Our model has no asset limit fields

novelFields.value: Administered locally via Aging and Disability Resource Centers (ADRCs) by county/tribe; different agencies/providers (e.g., IRIS Consultant Agencies (ICAs), Fiscal Employment Agents (FEAs)); wait times vary by region due to enrollment caps[1][6][7]
novelFields.note: Program varies by region — our model doesn't capture this

novelFields.value: Waitlists exist due to limited enrollment; varies by region and priority (e.g., higher priority for those in institutions or at risk)[1]
novelFields.note: Has waitlist info — our model has no wait time field

value: Proof of residency, income, assets, citizenship/immigration status, SSN, medical/functional assessment info; cooperate with verification requests[1][2]
novelFields.note: Has document checklist — our model doesn't store per-program documents

comparisons.dataShapeNotes: Self-directed budget model varies by functional need/priority tier via LTC FS assessment; county-administered via ADRCs with regional providers (ICAs/FEAs); no fixed income table—uses Medicaid standards; waitlisted with regional variations
gotchas: Must live in eligible community setting (not nursing home or certain facilities)—cannot enroll if currently institutionalized[1][3][7]
Self-directed model requires participant (or representative) to manage budget, hire workers, and use FEA—high responsibility level[3][7]
Enrollment capped with waitlists prioritized by need (e.g., institutional risk); not immediate access[1]
Tied to Medicaid eligibility—any changes in income/assets can affect status[1][2]
For 18-64 enrollees, services continue after 65, but initial target is elderly (65+) or disabled adults[2]

comparisons.name: Wisconsin Senior Employment Program (WISE)
comparisons.dataShapeNotes: Federally funded under Older Americans Act Title V (SCSEP); administered statewide via local workforce boards and subgrantees with varying contacts; priority tiers affect access; income tied to annual federal poverty levels (no fixed table in sources); no asset test
gotchas: Priority enrollment given to veterans, qualified spouses of veterans, and those over age 65—non-priority applicants may face delays or limited slots[2]
Positions are temporary, part-time training (not permanent jobs or full-time); designed as bridge to unsubsidized employment[1][2][3]
Must be actively unemployed and seeking work; not for current employees[1][2][4]
Income test strictly at 125% FPL—over-income disqualifies, and families must use current annual poverty guidelines[1][2][5]
Regional administration means contacting local provider essential; no centralized statewide application[1][2][3][4][6]
comparisons.officialSource: https://dwd.wisconsin.gov/ (Department of Workforce Development; specific WISE details via detwise@dwd.wisconsin.gov) or https://www.dhs.wisconsin.gov/publications/p00409-2024-2027.pdf (2024-2027 State Plan)[3][5]

comparisons.name: Wisconsin Home Energy Assistance Program (WHEAP)

diffs.found: One-time payment during heating season (Oct 1-May 15) covering portion of heating costs, non-heating electric costs, or energy crisis. Amount varies by household size, income, and energy costs; not full coverage. Additional: HE+ HVAC repairs/replacements for inoperable systems, HE+ Water Conservation for leaky fixtures/water heaters/toilets/piping (year-round), Weatherization referral for energy efficiency upgrades at no cost.[1][6]

novelFields.value: No asset limits mentioned in sources.
novelFields.note: Our model has no asset limit fields

novelFields.value: Administered by local WHEAP agencies with varying contact info/providers (e.g., Douglas County, Partners for Community Development in Sheboygan area); may have regional wait times or processes but no specific differences detailed.
novelFields.note: Program varies by region — our model doesn't capture this

novelFields.value: Not mentioned; high application volume noted.
novelFields.note: Has waitlist info — our model has no wait time field

value: SSN and DOB for all household members.
If renting: landlord name/address/phone or lease.
Gross income proof for prior month (pay stubs, tax docs, award letters for wages, SS/SSI/SSDI, pensions, veterans, child support, TANF/W2, etc.).
novelFields.note: Has document checklist — our model doesn't store per-program documents

comparisons.dataShapeNotes: Income limits provided as monthly/annual by household size with examples from different years; benefits as one-time variable payments plus referrals to weatherization/HVAC/water services; local agency administration statewide.
gotchas: One-time benefit per heating season only (Oct 1-May 15).
Payment covers portion, not full costs.
Must apply through local agency or online; incomplete apps denied after 30 days.
For crisis/disconnection, call immediately.
Income based on prior month (or prior year taxes for self-employed); provide 12-month energy history.
Weatherization/HE+ services referred separately if eligible.
Program year-specific income limits; check current via official site.
